coredata.dc:title: Long-lived phosphorescence emission of self-penetrating MOF based on 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene: Syntheses, structure and photoelectron performance
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiao-Gang
Lu, Xiao-Min
Zhai, Zhi-Min
Chang, Xin-Hong
Qin, Jian-Hua
Liu, Xin-Yi
Li, Fei-Fei
Ma, Lu-Fang
coredata.dc:description: One new binuclear Cd(II) based 3D metal-organic framework (MOF) [Cd(HBTC)(BIMB)] (1) (H3BTC ​= ​1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid, BIMB ​= ​1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene) was synthesized by the mixture of HBTC and BIMB ligands. X-ray single-crystal diffraction analysis indicates that the title MOF exhibits a 3D self-penetrating structure with the alternate arrangement of HBTC and BIMB ligands in spatial. Time-resolved measurements reveal that 1 exhibits prolonged phosphorescence lifetime more than 10 times in comparison with the pristine BIMB phosphor ligand. Theoretical calculations indicate that the HOMO and LUMO are completely separated and located on the BIMB and HBTC ligands, respectively, facilitating the generation of triplet state for long-lived phosphorescence emission. In addition, the photoelectron responds performance of 1 have also been studied.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Self-penetrating
Phosphorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The coordination environment of the Cd(II) center in 1. The twisty conformation (b) and coordination mode (c) of HBTC for 1. (d) View of 1D chain along [100] direction. Ball-and-stick (e) and schematic illustration (f) view of 3D network of 1.

(a) PXRD patterns of simulated (black) and as synthesized 1 (red). (b) thermogravimetric analysis curve of 1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Normalized fluorescent (black) and phosphorescent (red) spectra of BIMB (a), H3BTC (b) and 1 (c). Phosphorescent decay curves of BIMB (d), H3BTC (e) and 1 (f). All samples were measure in solid state at room temperature. Insets show the photographs of 1 under daylight and UV (365 ​nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The distributions of highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of 1.
The LSV curve (a) and transient current density–time curves (b) of 1 modified ITO in 0.5 ​M Na2SO4 aqueous solution without bias potential.

head.ce:title: Long-lived phosphorescence emission of self-penetrating MOF based on 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene: Syntheses, structure and photoelectron performance

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One new binuclear Cd(II) based 3D metal-organic framework (MOF) [Cd(HBTC)(BIMB)] (1) (H3BTC ​= ​1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid, BIMB ​= ​1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene) was synthesized by the mixture of HBTC and BIMB ligands. X-ray single-crystal diffraction analysis indicates that the title MOF exhibits a 3D self-penetrating structure with the alternate arrangement of HBTC and BIMB ligands in spatial. Time-resolved measurements reveal that 1 exhibits prolonged phosphorescence lifetime more than 10 times in comparison with the pristine BIMB phosphor ligand. Theoretical calculations indicate that the HOMO and LUMO are completely separated and located on the BIMB and HBTC ligands, respectively, facilitating the generation of triplet state for long-lived phosphorescence emission. In addition, the photoelectron responds performance of 1 have also been studied.

One new binuclear Cd(II) based 3D self-penetrating MOF was successfully synthesized. The combination of experiments and theoretical calculations indicate that the strategy of mixture-ligands can highly enhance the phosphorescent emission under room-temperature.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have become a research hotspot due to their potential applications in the fields of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [1,2], bioimaging [3], chemical sensing [4], security protection [5] and so on. Most known early discussion on phosphorescence materials focused on noble-metal (such as Pt, Ir and Ru) complexes [6–10]. Benefit from the metal−ligand charge transfer (MLCT) in these complexes, strong spin−orbit coupling promote intersystem crossing (ISC), facilitating efficient phosphorescence emission [11]. However, considering their impact on environment and expensive synthetic cost of heavy-metal complexes for phosphorescent emission, it is urgently desirable for the construction of new type of low-cost and stable materials with high phosphorescence performance at ambient conditions, developing their potential applications in optoelectronic fields.

Recently, significant progress has been made in obtaining long-lived RTP systems, which can be achieved by means of crystallization [12–14], H-aggregation [15,16], host-guest doping [17,18], the formation of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [19–25] and so on. Two key factors are considered necessary when RTP materials are designed: one is to facilitate the singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing (ISC) to generate the efficient triplet state, and the other is to restrain the maximum nonradiative transition pathways [12,15]. Hence, the construction of MOFs is used as an efficient way to generate a rigid environment to inhabit the nonradiative decay, enhancing the RTP emission. Moreover, MOFs formed by organic ligands coordinate with metal ion/cluster units exhibit structure tunability and facile functionalization [26–33]. In the MOF structures, the intermolecular interactions can be tuned effectively based on the appropriate selection of metal ions and ligands, which can act as model systems to understand structure and long-lived excited states relationship [23]. However, to date, only a few studies investigated MOFs based RTP materials [19–25], problems of unclear luminescent mechanism and short lifetime need to be solved. Therefore, obtaining RTP materials based on highly tunable structures and facile synthesis routes of MOFs still remain challenging.

Generally speaking, the suitable selection of phosphorescent ligands play key role in achieving MOFs with high RTP performance. Up to now, several aromatic carboxylate and nitrogen heterocyclic phosphorescent ligands have been utilized to construct MOFs with tunable RTP performance. The assemble of mixed-ligands and metal ions can result in greater tunability of structural frameworks and photophysical properties than that present with single ligands. However, the strategy of mixed-ligands MOFs have not yet been used in the formation of RTP materials.

In this work, we have developed a new Cd-based MOF [Cd(HBTC)(BIMB)] (1) with high-efficiency RTP from the mixture of poly-carboxylate ligand 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) and a flexible N-heterocyclic ligand 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene (BIMB). According to previous papers, organic molecules containing N-heteroatom have lone-pair electrons to promote the spin-orbital coupling for efficient triplet exciton generation [34–36]. Meanwhile, flexible ligands showing twisty spatial conformation can promote the intersystem crossing between the S1 and T1 excited states for phosphorescence emission. Based on above consideration, BIMB have been firstly proved to show green RTP in our experiments. And then, combination with HBTC, the title MOF 1 with prolonged RTP was obtained. X-ray single-crystal diffraction analysis indicates that as-synthesized MOF is a 3D self-penetrating structure, in which, the HBTC and BIMB ligands show alternate arrangement in spatial. Interestingly, 1 exhibits highly prolonged phosphorescent emission in comparison with the pristine BIMP phosphorescent ligand. Furthermore, the electronic structure and electronic transition behavior of 1 have also been investigated by theoretical calculation and photoelectric test.

Analytically pure Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Na2SO4, 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC), and 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene (BIMB) were purchased from Energy Chemical Co. Ltd. and used without further purification.

PXRD patterns was collected on a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ ​= ​1.5418 ​Å). Measurements were made in a 2θ range of 5–50° at room temperature with a step of 0.02° (2θ) and a counting time of 0.2 ​s/step. The operating power was 40 ​KV, 40 ​mA. The C, H, N analyses were carried out using a Perkin–Elmer Elementarvario elemental analysis instrument. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were carried out using SII EXSTAR6000 TG/DTA6300 thermal analyzer from room temperature to 800 ​°C under a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 ​°C min−1. The IR spectra were recorded in the range of 4000–400 ​cm−1 on a Nicolet 6700 (Thermo) FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. Room temperature photoluminescence spectra and time-resolved lifetime were conducted on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon arc lamp (Xe900), nanosecond flash-lamp (nF900) and a microsecond flashlamp with time-resolved single photon counting‒multi-channel scaling (MCS) mode.

Optoelectronic measurements were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua Co., Shanghai, China) by using MOF modified ITO as the working electrode (working area 1.0 ​cm2), Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, platinum wire electrode as a counter electrode. All electrochemical tests were performed at room temperature in 0.5 ​M Na2SO4 solution. The system was conducted in a quartz glass reactor ca. 50 ​cm3, and irradiated by a 300 ​W Xe lamp. All the linear-sweep voltammograms (LSV) with a scan rate of 50 ​mV/s. Transient photocurrent responses with the on–off cycle’s illumination without bias potential were tested in three-electrode system at ambient pressure and room temperature.

A mixture of Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (1 ​mmol, 0.31 ​g), 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (0.5 ​mmol, 0.11g), 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene (1 ​mmol, 0.23 ​g) and 8 ​mL of H2O was stirred for 10 ​min. The mixture was then transferred and sealed into a Teflon reactor (23 ​mL), and heated at 150 ​°C for 72 ​h. After that, the mixture was cooled at a rate of 5 ​°C·h−1 to room temperature. The light yellow block crystals of 1 were washed by ethanol (3 ​× ​10 ​mL), and dried in air at room temperature (yield: 58% based on Cd). Anal. Calc. (%) for C23H18CdN4O6: C 49.43, H 3.25, N 10.03; found (%): C 49.81, H 3.12, N 10.41. IR (cm−1): 3432 ​m, 2921 ​m, 1614 ​s, 1571 ​m, 1429 ​m, 1353 ​s, 1022 ​m, 773 ​m.

Single-crystal X-ray diffraction data for 1 was collected at room temperature (293K) on an Oxford Diffraction SuperNova area-detector diffractometer using mirror optics monochromated Mo Kα radiation (λ ​= ​0.71073 ​Å). CrysAlisPro [37] was used for the data collection, data reduction and empirical absorption correction. The crystal structure was solved by direct methods, using SHELXS-2014 and least-squares refined with SHELXL-2014 [38,39] using anisotropic thermal displacement parameters for all non-hydrogen atoms. The crystallographic data for 1 is listed in Table 1 . CCDC No. 1963937 contains the supplementary crystallographic data for 1.

The electronic structures of the title MOF were measured through periodic density functional theory (PDFT) method based on Dmol3 module in Material Studio software package [40–42]. The crystallographic information file (cif) of 1 was directly used for all of calculations. The initial configuration was fully optimized by PW91, GGA method with the double numerical basis sets plus polarization function (DNP).

10 ​mg crystal sample of 1 was grinded to powder and dispersed in 1 ​mL anhydrous ethanol by ultrasonicating for 30 ​min to form suspension liquid. 0.2 ​mL above suspension was dropped onto the surface of the pre-treated ITO substrate (1 ​× ​4 ​cm2) by controlling the coating area about 1 ​cm2, and allowing it to dry at room temperature.

The X-ray single crystal structural analyses reveals that 1 is a 3D self-penetrating framework, which crystallizes in the triclinic system with the space group Pī and the asymmetric unit contains one Cd(II) atom, one HBTC ligand and one BIMB ligand. As shown in Fig. 1 a, each Cd(II) atom in 1 exhibits a distorted pentagonal bipyramidal coordination environment, composed of chelated five carboxylic oxygen atoms from three BTC ligands, together with two nitrogen atoms belonging to two different BIMB ligands.

In 1, to fit the geometric requirements of the Cd(II) atoms, the HBTC ligand shows a twisty conformation between carboxylate groups and the central phenyl ring with the dihedral angle of 87.9°, 23.8° and 19.1°, respectively (Fig. 1b). As a result, it connects three Cd(II) atoms through two monodentrate and one chelated-monodentrate carboxylate groups (Fig. 1c). Based on such coordination fashion, the neighboring Cd(II) atoms are bridged by HBTC to form a 1D chain along the [100] direction with Cd⋯Cd distance of 3.809 ​Å between binuclear Cd(II) unit (Fig. 1d). In addition, the 1D chains are further extended by BIMB ligands along bc plan to generate 3D structure (Fig. 1e). Herein, the BIMB ligand also features a twisty conformation between two outer imidazole rings and the central phenyl ring with the dihedral angle of 78.84°. From the viewpoint of topology, the overall 3D network of 1 can be simplified as a six-connected net based on the six-connected binuclear Cd(II) unit. Significantly, this 3D architecture shows a self-penetrating pattern, in which the shortest eight-numbered loop consisting of four binuclear Cd(II) nodes, two HBTC and two BIMB linkers, is interpenetrated by the other one via coordination bonds (Fig. 1f).

The phase purity of the title MOF was confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction analyses (PXRD) for further photophysical properties and photoelectric responds measurements. The simulated and as synthesized one are shown in Fig. 2 a. The result reveals that all main diffractive peaks of the powder sample matches well with the simulated pattern one from the single-crystal diffraction data, suggesting the as-synthesized sample is single phase. The thermal stabilities of 1 was conformed by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) as shown in Fig. 2b. The TGA curve indicates that the framework of 1 can be stable up to about 240 ​°C.

To investigate the photophysical properties of the pristine H3BTC, BIMB and 1, both steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy in the solid state were measured at room temperature. The pristine BIMB ligand presents fluorescence emission at 455 ​nm (λex ​= ​370 ​nm). Upon excitation at 360 ​nm by a microsecond flash lamp (100 ​Hz), the phosphorescence emission peaked at 533 ​nm (Fig. 3 a) can be detected. Differently, the fluorescent spectra of free H3BTC consists of a strong band centered at 513 and 538 ​nm together with one weak shoulder around 398 ​nm. The phosphorescent spectra of H3BTC maintains the same emission peaks, however, the weak shoulder at 398 ​nm obviously decrease (Fig. 3b).

Upon coordination with Cd(II) ions through a hydrothermal conditions, complex 1 exhibits distinct photoluminescence in comparison with the pure BIMB and H3BTC ligand. As shown in Fig. 3c, complex 1 presents light blue emission at 436 and 478 ​nm with the excitation at 358 ​nm. Measured under phosphorescence mode, broad phosphorescence emission band located at 527 ​nm (λex ​= ​350 ​nm) can be detected. Insets are the photos of 1 radiated by daylight and UV (365 ​nm). The phosphorescence decay curves (Fig. 3d ​~ ​f) give a longer lifetime value of 46 ​ms, which is about 10 times as long as that of the pristine BIMB. It concluded that coordination of the ligand to the metal ions increases the rigidity of the ligand and reduces the loss of energy through radiation less pathways, enhancing the phosphorescent emission in the metal-organic hybrid system.

To better demonstrate the photophysical properties of 1, we carried out the period density functional theoretical (PDFT) calculations from the crystallographic information file (cif) directly. As shown in Fig. 4 , it can be obviously observed that the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) are mainly disperse in two imidazole rings of BIMB ligands, while the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) are fully located on the HBTC ligand. Thus, the title MOF 1 displays a new type of molecular orbitals disjunction between carboxylate ligand and N-heterocyclic ligand, which is completely different from previous reported RTP MOFs.

In the single ligand based Zn-iso-phthalate MOF [23], the HOMOs and LUMOs are separated between the carboxylate groups and the phenyl ring in the same IPA ligand owing to their large torsion angle (39.02°). While in the reported cluster based Zn-EtOIPA [22] and MOF-5 [19], the HOMOs and LUMOs mainly disperse in the hexa-/teranuclear zinc clusters and the 5-ethoxyisophthalate/terephthalate ligands, respectively. Herein, the complete separation of HOMOs and LUMOs in the mixture-ligands MOFs provides a new strategy to design long-last RTP materials.

To further understand the electron behavior of luminescent materials, photoelectron responds of 1 modified ITO electrode were studied in 0.5 ​M Na2SO4 aqueous solution in a standard three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the linear sweep voltammetry curve (LSV) reveals that 1 shows enhanced photocurrent with the increase of negative voltage. The transient photocurrent responses with the on–off cycle’s illumination in the above three-electrode system reveal an unusual optoelectronic behaviors (Fig. 5b). It was observed that 1 shows photocurrent density about −110 nA when the light source was turned on without applying an external potential. Abnormally, high photocurrent density up to −135 nA was detected when the light source is switched off, and then decreases gradually to −79 nA. However, when the light switched on again, it reduces to −48 nA instantly and enhances to −110 nA gradually. The reversible change in transient photocurrent density can be readily repeated at least for 10 times during on–off cycles of illumination. Such unusual photocurrent responses behavior may be assigned to the long-lived triplet excited states of 1, which could enable excited-state migration over longer distances, generating more free charges.

In summary, a Cd(II) based metal-organic framework (MOF) [Cd(HBTC)(BIMB)] (1) has been synthesized based on the mixture of poly-carboxylate ligand 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) and a flexible N-heterocyclic ligand 1,4-bis(imidazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene (BIMB). Crystal structure reveals that 1 has a self-penetrating 3D six-connected network based on binuclear Cd(II) node. The experiment and period density functional theoretical calculations indicate that complete separation of HOMOs and LUMOs in the mixture-ligands of 1 can efficiently restrain the recombination of electron-hole, promote long-lived phosphorescent emission. Therefore, this work provides a new strategy to design long-last RTP materials stem from the mixture-ligands MOFs system, more MOF materials with high phosphorescent performance will be developed in our ongoing work.
X.-G. Yang and X.-H. Chang conceived the idea and designed research; X.-M. Lu, Z.-M. Zhai, and X.-Y. Liu synthesized and characterized materials; all authors analyzed data and wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21971100, 21771097 and U1504202), Project of Central Plains Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents of Henan Province, and Key Scientific Research Projects of Higher Education of Henan Province (No. 20A150005).
